text.title: Core Quality Attributes: Performance, Scalability, Availability, Reliability
text.summary: Definitions, measurable metrics, and architectural tactics for the four most architecture-critical quality attributes. Every attribute must have a concrete numeric threshold to be actionable.

rule.statement: Define performance requirements as p50/p95/p99 latency targets and minimum throughput at a specified load, not as "the system should be fast". Measure under realistic load, not idle.
rule.rationale: Vague performance requirements cannot be validated or used as architecture selection criteria. p99 latency is the user experience for the slowest 1 in 100 requests — consistently worse than p95 and the real bound for SLA compliance.

list.item: p50 latency (median): target for average user experience — typical target <100ms for web APIs
p95 latency: target for most users — typical target <300ms
p99 latency: tail latency bound — typical target <1000ms; drives SLA commitments
Throughput: requests per second at target latency — must specify both metrics together
TTFB (Time to First Byte): critical for perceived page load — target <200ms
Tactic: caching (Redis, CDN, browser cache) — eliminates repeated computation
Tactic: connection pooling — prevents per-request connection overhead
Tactic: async processing — defer non-critical work to background queues
Tactic: read replicas — distribute read load across multiple database instances
Tactic: response compression (gzip/brotli) — reduce payload size on the wire

rule.statement: Design for horizontal scalability from the start: stateless application tier, externalized session storage, database connection pooling. Vertical scaling is a one-time emergency measure, not a strategy.
rule.rationale: Vertical scaling has a hard ceiling (the largest available instance) and requires downtime on most platforms. Horizontal scaling requires stateless services — retrofitting statelessness into a stateful application is expensive.

list.item: Vertical: add CPU/RAM to existing instance — simple, limited ceiling, requires downtime
Horizontal: add more instances behind a load balancer — requires stateless services, unlimited ceiling
Elastic: auto-scale instances based on load metrics — cloud-native, cost-optimal for variable traffic
Stateless requirement: no in-memory session state; sessions stored in Redis or a distributed cache

rule.statement: Define availability as a specific SLA percentage with an annual downtime budget. 99.9% allows 8.7 hours/year; 99.99% allows 52 minutes/year. Every additional "nine" requires significantly more architectural investment.
rule.rationale: Without a specific availability target, engineering effort is unconstrained. The jump from 99.9% to 99.99% requires active/active multi-region, zero-downtime deploys, and automated failover — all expensive. Align the target with business impact.

list.item: 99% SLA: 87.6 hours downtime/year — acceptable for internal tools
99.9% SLA: 8.7 hours downtime/year — standard for SaaS products
99.99% SLA: 52 minutes downtime/year — requires active/active redundancy
99.999% SLA: 5 minutes downtime/year — carrier-grade, extremely expensive
MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures): average time system runs before failure
MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery): average time to restore service after failure — target <15 min for 99.9%
Tactic: health checks + automated restart (readiness/liveness probes)
Tactic: blue-green or canary deployments to reduce deployment downtime
Tactic: circuit breakers to prevent cascade failures
Tactic: database replication with automatic failover (RDS Multi-AZ, Patroni)
